main_event: Two civilian witnesses in San Antonio, Texas observed a single orange bean-shaped object at approximately 2230 CST on 7 September 1952; one witness (a chemist) observed from the NW sky at ~3 miles / 9000' altitude and it remained motionless for 15-20 seconds before disappearing; a second witness from a different location observed an explosive-like orange flash in the East sky for ~3 seconds at estimated 15 miles / 20,000' altitude.
sighted_object: bean shaped; orange/cherry-orange color; size ranged from pea to small dirigible depending on observer; motionless; no sound; no smoke; no pulsation; disappeared like a light going out; estimated flare-equivalent temperature of ~2000°F (chemist witness)
length_of_observation: approximately 15-20 seconds (chemist witness); approximately 3 seconds (second witness); teletype reports 25 seconds and 5 seconds for two observers
witness_description: Primary: Dr. [REDACTED], chemist, and wife (NW sky; 15-20 seconds; chemist applied spectral/temperature analysis); Secondary: [REDACTED] and wife (E sky; ~3 seconds; thought object might be exploding aircraft); report filed by Major Robert W. Fox, Asst Air Provost Marshal, Kelly AFB
interesting_points: Chemist witness applied technical analysis: estimated object color equivalent to flare temperature of ~2000°F; speculated about ionization of air as possible explanation
Two observer groups with significantly different data: NW sky at 9000' (3 miles) vs E sky at 20,000' (15 miles) — either same object seen from very different angles or two separate events
Duration discrepancy: chemist says 15-20 seconds; second witness says ~3 seconds; teletype reports 25 seconds and 5 seconds (possibly two separate observers' durations cited)
Second witness compared to exploding aircraft — noted on record card; also said appeared 'explosive-like'
Record card source field reads 'Chemist, et al.' — unusual sourcing notation acknowledging professional expertise
Weather was rain with scattered clouds at time of sighting — makes astronomical and reflective explanations less likely
Conclusion left as Unknown — no satisfactory explanation found despite detailed chemist testimony
